one_line_takeaway: SecInfer turns inference-time scaling into a prompt-injection defense by sampling diverse chain-of-thought responses and then selecting the one that best fits the target task (not the majority), pushing attack success to near zero across models, attacks, and agents where self-consistency and even GCG-fragile fine-tuning defenses fail.
key_finding: Across all 4 backend LLMs, 6 target tasks, and 7 existing attacks, SecInfer drives ASR to nearly zero while preserving utility (e.g., GPT-4.1 on AG News: U 0.87 with defense vs 0.86 undefended). It beats prior inference-time-scaling methods that fail against injection: under Combined Attack on LLaMA3.1-8B (AG News), self-consistency yields UA 0.22 / ASR 0.35 whereas SecInfer yields UA 0.81 / ASR 0.00, at comparable cost. Against optimization-based GCG (LLaMA), the state-of-the-art fine-tuning defense MetaSecAlign is defeated (high ASR / low UA) while SecInfer holds ASR near 0; SecInfer thus remains robust in both the CA and GCG regimes where each strong baseline (MetaSecAlign for GCG, most pre-processing defenses generally) breaks. It generalizes to agents: InjecAgent ASR drops from 0.37/0.40 (Direct-Harm/Data-Stealing base) and 0.61/0.68 (enhanced) to <=0.02; AgentDojo ASR from 0.21 to 0.02. SecInfer also withstands all 6 adaptive attacks (ASR stays low even when UA degrades).
core_contribution: SecInfer is a prevention-based defense against prompt injection that recasts inference-time scaling as a security mechanism. Its own contribution is a two-step pipeline: (Step I) system-prompt-guided sampling, which generates N candidate responses by randomly drawing from a hand-crafted set of chain-of-thought system prompts (combined with temperature and top-k stochastic decoding) so the backend LLM explores diverse reasoning paths rather than relying only on decoding randomness; and (Step II) target-task-guided aggregation, which selects the candidate most aligned with the intended target task rather than the majority. For closed-domain tasks Step II maps each response into the valid output domain, discards out-of-domain responses, and takes a majority vote; for open-domain tasks it embeds responses (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), applies non-parametric agglomerative clustering, picks each cluster's centroid-nearest representative, and uses a judge LLM (given the target instruction) to select the representative that best fulfills the target task or return a fallback. The authors' key insight, distinct from cited inference-time-scaling work, is that prompt injection produces correlated (not independent) errors across samples, so majority-vote methods like self-consistency fail; grounding aggregation in the target task instead of frequency is what makes scaling work as a defense.
threat_model: Prevention of prompt injection attacks on LLM-integrated applications and agents. A strong, adaptive attacker is assumed to have full knowledge of the LLM system (target instruction, target data, backend LLM parameters) AND of the defense itself (the sampling and aggregation algorithms and the judge LLM parameters). The attacker can contaminate only the untrusted target data (e.g., web pages, reviews, tool-call results) by embedding an injected prompt; it cannot alter the system prompt, target instruction, or backend LLM. Notably the authors keep the Step I system prompts confidential as part of the defense (Adaptive Attack VI shows utility drops when the attacker also knows them). Goals: preserve utility under no attack (U), preserve utility under attack (UA), and reduce attack success rate (ASR).
limitations: (1) By design SecInfer is ineffective when the injected task and target task are the SAME type sharing one closed output domain, because the attacker-desired output is still consistent with the target task; e.g., sentiment-analysis-into-sentiment-analysis ASR only falls from 0.90 to 0.79, since this case reduces to a traditional adversarial example. (2) Utility under attack can degrade even when ASR stays low: Adaptive Attack V, which GCG-optimizes the ENTIRE contaminated data (destroying the target data), drives UA to near 0 on several tasks (no defense can preserve utility if the target data is fully overwritten), though ASR remains ~0. (3) Computational overhead: ~0.612s per sample vs 0.087s undefended and 0.075s for MetaSecAlign, roughly a 7x cost, mitigated by parallelizing Step I (0.221s per sample across 5 GH200 GPUs). (4) Effectiveness depends on judge-LLM strength: a weak judge (LLaMA3.1-8B) gives U 0.53 / UA 0.45 vs GPT-4.1 judge U 0.75 / UA 0.71. (5) Security partly relies on keeping the Step I system prompts secret. (6) Slight UA-below-U gaps persist because contaminated data can still perturb outputs without triggering the injected task.

datasets_used: AG News (closed-domain target task, 4-way topic classification)
NarrativeQA (open-domain target task, reading comprehension QA)
Injected tasks: MRPC, RTE, HSOL, SMS-SPAM, SST2, Jfleg, Gigaword (Open-Prompt-Injection) plus the 'COMPROMISE' injected task (Zaremba et al.)
quantitative_results: SecInfer reduces ASR to ~0.00 across all 4 backend LLMs, 6 target tasks and 7 existing attacks (Table 1)
Utility preserved: GPT-4.1 on AG News U=0.87 with SecInfer vs 0.86 undefended
Combined Attack, LLaMA3.1-8B, AG News: SecInfer UA=0.81 / ASR=0.00 vs self-consistency UA=0.22 / ASR=0.35 (Table 3)
GCG attack, LLaMA3.1-8B: MetaSecAlign defeated (high ASR / low UA) while SecInfer keeps ASR ~0.00-0.02 (Table 2)
MetaSecAlign+SecInfer combination further boosts UA (e.g., SQuAD_v2 UA 0.65 under GCG)
InjecAgent: ASR (Direct-Harm/Data-Stealing) 0.37/0.40 base and 0.61/0.68 enhanced -> 0.00/0.02 and 0.01/0.02 with SecInfer (Table 5)

Inference time: 0.612s/sample (SecInfer) vs 0.087s (no defense), 0.622s (self-consistency), 0.719s (best-of-N), 0.075s (MetaSecAlign); 0.221s/sample when Step I parallelized over 5 GH200 GPUs (Table 4)
Judge-LLM ablation: LLaMA3.1-8B judge U=0.53/UA=0.45/ASR=0.04; Qwen3-8B U=0.72/UA=0.64/ASR=0.00; GPT-4.1 U=0.75/UA=0.71/ASR=0.00 (Table 7)
Same-type limitation: sentiment-into-sentiment ASR 0.90 -> 0.79 after SecInfer
Adaptive Attack V (rewrites entire data): UA collapses to ~0 on several tasks but ASR stays ~0 (Table 8)
Memorization test: >94% of judge outputs have string-similarity ratio <0.6 to ground truth
Default N=5 sufficient to drive ASR to 0; ASR converges to 0 faster than UA saturates as N grows
baselines_compared: Prevention defenses: Delimiters, Sandwich prevention, Instructional prevention, Paraphrasing, Retokenization, DataSentinel+PromptLocate (DS+PL), PromptArmor, MetaSecAlign
Inference-time-scaling methods: in-context learning ICL-2 / ICL-4, chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, iterative refinement (IR), best-of-N (BoN), self-consistency (SC)
